cases.title: Seat-Belt Sign and Hidden Bleeding
cases.presentation: A restrained 35-year-old in a high-speed MVC has a bruise across her lower abdomen from the lap belt. She is mildly tender but says she feels 'okay.' Her heart rate is climbing and her skin is becoming cool.
cases.question: How should you proceed?
options: Treat as potential intra-abdominal injury: oxygen, IV en route, keep warm, rapid transport to a trauma center
Wait for the abdomen to become rigid before acting
cases.explanation: A seat-belt sign with rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, and cool skin signals possible intra-abdominal injury and early shock—even with a benign-feeling exam. Treat for shock and transport rapidly; do not wait for rigidity.
cases.clinicalPearl: A seat-belt sign plus early shock signs is intra-abdominal injury until proven otherwise—mechanism beats a reassuring exam.

cases.title: Evisceration After a Stabbing
cases.presentation: A young man was stabbed in the abdomen and has a loop of bowel protruding through the wound. He is anxious and in pain.
cases.question: What is the correct wound management?
options: Gently push the bowel back into the abdomen
Cover the exposed bowel with a moist sterile dressing and an occlusive layer; treat for shock and transport
Apply a dry tight pressure dressing
Pack the wound with hemostatic gauze around the bowel
cases.explanation: Never replace eviscerated organs. Cover them with a moist sterile dressing and an occlusive layer to keep them warm and moist, prevent contamination, treat for shock, and transport. Flexing the knees may reduce tension.
cases.clinicalPearl: Moist, cover, transport—never replace eviscerated organs into the abdomen.

cases.title: Unstable Pelvis in Shock
cases.presentation: A pedestrian struck by a car has pelvic pain and is in shock. On a single gentle assessment the pelvis feels unstable. There is no external bleeding.
cases.question: What is the priority intervention?
options: Spring the pelvis several times to confirm instability
Apply a pelvic binder at the greater trochanters, treat for shock, and transport rapidly
Delay transport to obtain IV access
cases.explanation: An unstable pelvis with shock indicates major pelvic hemorrhage. Apply a pelvic binder at the level of the greater trochanters to close the pelvic volume and tamponade bleeding, treat for shock, and transport rapidly to surgical care. Avoid repeated pelvic manipulation.
cases.clinicalPearl: Bind early, assess once: a pelvic binder at the trochanters can be life-saving—repeated springing only worsens bleeding.
